title: Seated Spinal Twist
description: A gentle seated twist to relieve lower back tension from prolonged sitting. This movement helps restore spinal mobility and can reduce stiffness that accumulates during long work sessions.
steps: Sit upright in your chair with feet flat on the floor.
Place your right hand on the outside of your left knee.
Place your left hand on the back of your chair or armrest.
Gently twist your torso to the left, looking over your left shoulder.
Hold for 15-20 seconds, breathing deeply.
Return to center and repeat on the other side.
precautions: Avoid if you have herniated discs
Do not force the twist beyond comfort

title: Neck Rolls
description: Gentle circular neck movements to release tension in the cervical spine and surrounding muscles. Particularly effective for those who hold their phone between ear and shoulder.
steps: Sit or stand with a straight spine.
Slowly drop your chin toward your chest.
Roll your head gently to the right, bringing your ear toward your shoulder.
Continue rolling your head back (gently), then to the left shoulder.
Complete the circle back to the starting position.
Perform 3 slow circles in each direction.
precautions: Move slowly to avoid dizziness
Skip the backward extension if you have cervical issues

title: Wrist Circles and Flexion Stretches
description: A combination of wrist rotations and stretches to prevent repetitive strain injury from keyboard and mouse use. Essential for programmers and heavy typists.
steps: Extend both arms in front of you.
Make fists and rotate your wrists in circles, 10 times clockwise.
Reverse direction for 10 counter-clockwise circles.
Open your hands and spread your fingers wide for 5 seconds.
Extend one arm, palm up. Use the other hand to gently pull fingers toward you.
Hold for 15 seconds, then switch hands.
precautions: Stop if you feel sharp pain
Consult a doctor if you have existing carpal tunnel

title: Shoulder Shrugs
description: Simple shoulder elevation and release to quickly dissipate tension that builds up in the trapezius muscles from hunching over a keyboard.
steps: Sit or stand with arms relaxed at your sides.
Inhale and raise both shoulders up toward your ears as high as possible.
Hold the raised position for 3-5 seconds, squeezing the muscles.
Exhale and drop your shoulders down completely, releasing all tension.
Repeat 8-10 times.
precautions: Avoid if you have acute shoulder injury

title: Chest Opener Stretch
description: A standing or seated stretch that opens the chest and counteracts the forward-rounding posture caused by working at a computer all day.
steps: Stand up or sit at the edge of your chair.
Clasp your hands behind your back, interlacing your fingers.
Straighten your arms and gently lift them away from your body.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together and open your chest.
Lift your chin slightly and hold for 20-30 seconds.
Release and repeat 2-3 times.
precautions: Do not force arms too high if shoulders are tight

title: 4-7-8 Breathing Technique
description: A structured breathing pattern developed by Dr. Andrew Weil that activates the parasympathetic nervous system. Particularly effective for calming anxiety before presentations or during high-stress deadlines.
steps: Sit comfortably with your back straight.
Place the tip of your tongue behind your upper front teeth.
Exhale completely through your mouth, making a whoosh sound.
Close your mouth and inhale quietly through your nose for 4 counts.
Hold your breath for 7 counts.
Exhale completely through your mouth for 8 counts.
Repeat the cycle 3-4 times.
precautions: May cause lightheadedness initially
Start with 2 cycles if new to breathwork
source: Dr. Andrew Weil's integrative medicine research

title: Desk Push-Ups
description: Modified push-ups using your desk as support. A quick way to get blood flowing and activate major muscle groups without leaving your workspace.
steps: Stand facing your desk, about arm's length away.
Place your hands on the edge of the desk, shoulder-width apart.
Step your feet back until your body forms a straight line.
Lower your chest toward the desk by bending your elbows.
Push back up to the starting position.
Perform 10-15 repetitions.
precautions: Ensure desk is stable and won't slide
Skip if you have wrist pain
source: Fitness trainer recommendations for office workers

title: Eye Palming
description: A relaxation technique for strained eyes that blocks all light and provides warmth. Used by yogis and recommended by ophthalmologists for screen-fatigued eyes.
steps: Rub your palms together vigorously for 10 seconds to generate warmth.
Close your eyes gently.
Cup your warm palms over your closed eyes without pressing on the eyeballs.
Rest your elbows on your desk for support.
Breathe deeply and focus on the complete darkness for 30-60 seconds.
Slowly remove your hands and blink gently before opening your eyes fully.
precautions: Do not press on your eyeballs
Remove contact lenses if uncomfortable

title: Seated Figure-4 Hip Stretch
description: A seated piriformis and hip stretch that addresses the tightness caused by prolonged sitting. Opens the outer hip and can relieve sciatic-like discomfort.
steps: Sit upright in your chair with feet flat on the floor.
Cross your right ankle over your left knee, forming a figure-4 shape.
Keep your right foot flexed to protect the knee.
Sit tall and gently lean forward from the hips until you feel a stretch in your right hip.
Hold for 20-30 seconds.
Switch legs and repeat.
precautions: Avoid if you have knee problems
Do not force the stretch

title: Seated Cat-Cow Stretch
description: An adapted version of the yoga cat-cow pose performed while seated. Mobilizes the entire spine and relieves compression from sitting in one position too long.
steps: Sit at the edge of your chair with feet flat on the floor.
Place your hands on your knees.
Inhale: arch your back, push your chest forward, look up slightly (cow).
Exhale: round your back, tuck your chin to your chest, pull your belly in (cat).
Flow smoothly between the two positions.
Repeat 8-10 times, synchronized with your breathing.
precautions: Move within a pain-free range
Avoid if you have acute back injury

title: Levator Scapulae Stretch
description: Targets the levator scapulae muscle that runs from the neck to the shoulder blade. This muscle is a primary source of 'tech neck' pain for office workers.
steps: Sit upright and turn your head 45 degrees to the right.
Look down toward your right armpit.
Place your right hand on the back of your head and apply gentle downward pressure.
You should feel the stretch on the left side of your neck, toward the shoulder blade.
Hold for 20-30 seconds.
Repeat on the other side.
precautions: Apply pressure gently
Stop if you feel numbness or tingling

title: Box Breathing
description: A breathing technique used by Navy SEALs and first responders to maintain calm under pressure. Involves equal counts for inhale, hold, exhale, and hold. Excellent for high-stakes work environments.
steps: Sit comfortably with your back straight and feet on the floor.
Slowly exhale all the air from your lungs.
Inhale through your nose for a slow count of 4.
Hold your breath for a count of 4.
Exhale through your mouth for a count of 4.
Hold your breath (lungs empty) for a count of 4.
Repeat for 4-6 cycles.
precautions: Do not practice while driving
Reduce hold time if you feel dizzy

title: Standing Calf Raises
description: Simple calf raises that can be done while standing at your desk, in a meeting, or waiting for code to compile. Promotes blood flow in the lower legs and prevents pooling.
steps: Stand behind your chair and lightly hold the back for balance.
Rise up onto the balls of your feet, lifting your heels as high as possible.
Hold the top position for 2 seconds.
Slowly lower back down.
Repeat 15-20 times.
For added difficulty, do one leg at a time.
precautions: Hold onto something for balance
Avoid if you have ankle injuries

title: Doorway Chest Stretch
description: Uses a doorway to deeply stretch the pectoral muscles and front of shoulders. One of the most effective stretches for reversing computer posture.
steps: Stand in a doorway with your arms raised to 90 degrees, elbows at shoulder height.
Place your forearms against the door frame on each side.
Step one foot forward through the doorway.
Lean your body forward gently until you feel a stretch across your chest.
Hold for 20-30 seconds.
Adjust arm height higher or lower to target different chest fibers.
precautions: Do not bounce or push too hard
Avoid if you have shoulder impingement

title: Finger Tendon Glides
description: A sequence of hand positions that systematically glide the finger tendons through their sheaths. Prevents adhesions and maintains hand dexterity for keyboard workers.
steps: Start with your hand open, fingers straight and together.
Bend your fingers at the middle and end joints into a hook fist (fingertips touch palm base).
Return to straight fingers.
Make a full fist, wrapping fingers tightly.
Return to straight fingers.
Touch fingertips to the base of your palm (tabletop position).
Return to straight. Repeat the full sequence 5 times per hand.
precautions: Move smoothly without forcing
Stop if you feel clicking or pain

title: Upper Trapezius Stretch
description: Targets the upper trapezius, the most commonly tense muscle in desk workers. This stretch provides immediate relief from the 'shoulders up to ears' tension pattern.
steps: Sit upright and grasp the bottom of your chair with your right hand.
Tilt your head to the left, bringing your left ear toward your left shoulder.
Use your left hand to apply gentle additional pressure on the right side of your head.
You should feel a stretch along the right side of your neck and into your shoulder.
Hold for 20-30 seconds.
Repeat on the other side.
precautions: Apply gentle pressure only
Avoid if you have cervical disc issues

title: Seated Hamstring Stretch
description: A seated stretch for tight hamstrings that contribute to lower back pain and posterior pelvic tilt. Essential for workers who sit for 8+ hours daily.
steps: Sit at the edge of your chair.
Extend your right leg straight out in front of you with your heel on the floor.
Keep your toes pointing up.
Sit tall and hinge forward from your hips (not your waist).
Reach toward your right foot until you feel a stretch behind your thigh.
Hold for 20-30 seconds. Repeat on the other side.
precautions: Keep your back straight while hinging
Do not round your spine

title: Diaphragmatic Breathing
description: Deep belly breathing that engages the diaphragm instead of shallow chest breathing. Most desk workers default to shallow breathing, which increases anxiety and reduces oxygen intake.
steps: Sit comfortably or recline slightly in your chair.
Place one hand on your chest and one on your belly.
Breathe in slowly through your nose for 4 seconds.
Focus on your belly rising (not your chest).
Exhale slowly through pursed lips for 6 seconds.
Your belly should fall while your chest stays relatively still.
Continue for 2-3 minutes.
precautions: May feel unusual at first if you are a chronic chest breather

title: Seated Torso Side Bend
description: A lateral stretch that targets the obliques and intercostal muscles between the ribs. Improves breathing capacity and relieves side body tension from asymmetric sitting.
steps: Sit upright with feet flat on the floor.
Raise your right arm overhead.
Lean your torso to the left, reaching your right hand over your head to the left side.
Keep both sitting bones firmly on the chair.
Feel the stretch along your right side.
Hold for 15-20 seconds. Repeat on the other side.
precautions: Keep the movement controlled
Avoid if you have rib injuries

title: Eye Focus Shifting
description: An exercise to strengthen the eye's focusing muscles (ciliary muscles) that become fatigued from staring at a fixed screen distance. Alternates focus between near and far objects.
steps: Hold your thumb about 10 inches from your face.
Focus on your thumb for 5 seconds, seeing it clearly.
Shift your focus to an object 20+ feet away (a window view or far wall).
Focus on the distant object for 5 seconds.
Shift back to your thumb.
Repeat 10 times.
Blink fully several times between shifts.
precautions: See an eye doctor if blurriness persists outside of screen use

title: Chair Squats
description: Stand up and sit down from your chair repeatedly -- a functional squat that activates the largest muscle groups in your body. Great for a quick metabolic boost.
steps: Stand in front of your chair with feet shoulder-width apart.
Extend your arms forward for balance.
Lower yourself slowly until you just touch the chair seat (don't fully sit).
Stand back up by pushing through your heels.
Keep your weight centered and knees tracking over toes.
Perform 10-15 repetitions.
precautions: Use the chair for support if needed
Avoid if you have acute knee pain

title: Thoracic Extension Over Chair
description: Uses the back of your chair to create a fulcrum for extending the thoracic spine (upper-mid back). Directly counteracts the forward-flexed posture of desk work.
steps: Scoot forward in your chair so the chair back hits your mid-back.
Interlace your hands behind your head, supporting your neck.
Slowly lean backward over the chair back, opening your chest toward the ceiling.
Breathe deeply and hold for 10-15 seconds.
Return to upright.
Repeat 3-5 times, moving the chair contact point slightly higher or lower each time.
precautions: Support your neck with your hands
Don't extend too far if it causes pain

title: Chin Tucks
description: Strengthens the deep cervical flexor muscles that keep your head balanced over your spine. The single most important exercise for correcting forward head posture ('tech neck').
steps: Sit or stand with your spine straight.
Look straight ahead with a neutral head position.
Gently draw your chin straight back, as if making a double chin.
You should feel a stretch at the base of your skull and the front of your neck engage.
Hold for 5 seconds.
Release and repeat 10-12 times.
precautions: Retract horizontally, do not tilt your head up or down

title: Seated Ankle Circles
description: Rotating the ankles to improve circulation in the lower legs and prevent the swelling and stiffness that comes from prolonged sitting. Can be done completely under the desk.
steps: Sit with feet flat on the floor.
Lift your right foot slightly off the ground.
Rotate your ankle clockwise in full circles, 10 times.
Reverse direction for 10 counter-clockwise circles.
Switch to the left foot and repeat.
Finish by flexing and pointing each foot 10 times.
precautions: Avoid large forceful circles if you have ankle instability

title: Prayer Stretch (Wrist Extensors)
description: A prayer-position stretch that targets the wrist extensors and flexors simultaneously. Quick and effective for keyboard warriors between typing sessions.
steps: Press your palms together in front of your chest in a prayer position.
Keep your palms pressed and slowly lower your hands toward your waist.
Stop when you feel a comfortable stretch in your wrists and forearms.
Hold for 15-20 seconds.
Then reverse: press the backs of your hands together, fingers pointing down.
Raise your hands slightly to stretch the opposite side. Hold 15-20 seconds.
precautions: Do not force hands lower than comfortable
source: Hand therapy exercises for repetitive strain

title: Wall Angels
description: A standing exercise performed against a wall that strengthens the postural muscles between the shoulder blades while stretching the chest. Like making a snow angel against a wall.
steps: Stand with your back flat against a wall, feet 6 inches from the wall.
Press your lower back, upper back, and head against the wall.
Raise your arms to a 'goal post' position (elbows at 90 degrees, backs of hands on wall).
Slowly slide your arms up the wall overhead, keeping contact with the wall.
Slide back down to the goal post position.
Perform 10-12 repetitions slowly.
precautions: If you cannot maintain wall contact, reduce range of motion

title: Nostril Breathing (Nadi Shodhana)
description: Alternate nostril breathing from the yoga tradition. Balances the left and right hemispheres of the brain and creates a deeply calming effect. Used in high-performance environments for focus.
steps: Sit comfortably with your spine erect.
Use your right thumb to close your right nostril.
Inhale slowly through your left nostril for 4 counts.
Close your left nostril with your ring finger, release your thumb.
Exhale through your right nostril for 4 counts.
Inhale through the right nostril for 4 counts.
Close right nostril, release left, exhale through left for 4 counts.
This is one complete cycle. Repeat 5-8 cycles.
precautions: Avoid during nasal congestion
Do not hold breath if you feel uncomfortable
source: Pranayama yoga tradition, supported by clinical research

title: Isometric Neck Strengthening
description: Uses your hand as resistance against your head to strengthen neck muscles without any visible movement. Completely discreet and can be done during meetings.
steps: Sit upright with a neutral head position.
Place your right hand against the right side of your head.
Push your head into your hand while your hand resists -- no movement should occur.
Hold for 8-10 seconds.
Repeat on the left side, front (hand on forehead), and back (hands behind head).
Do 3 sets in each direction.
precautions: Use moderate force, not maximum effort
Avoid if you have acute neck pain

title: Seated Pigeon Stretch
description: A deeper hip opener variation of the figure-4 stretch, targeting the piriformis and deep external rotators. Particularly helpful for those who cross their legs habitually.
steps: Sit at the edge of your chair.
Place your right ankle on your left knee.
Flex your right foot to protect the knee.
Sit tall, then lean forward from the hips until you feel a deep stretch.
For more intensity, gently press your right knee down with your right hand.
Hold for 30 seconds. Switch sides.
precautions: Do not force the knee down
Skip if you have knee injuries

title: Desk Plank Hold
description: A modified plank using your desk for support. Engages the entire core without getting on the floor. Builds the abdominal and back strength needed for good sitting posture.
steps: Place your forearms on the edge of your desk, shoulder-width apart.
Step your feet back until your body forms a straight line from head to heels.
Engage your core by pulling your belly button toward your spine.
Keep your hips level -- don't let them sag or pike up.
Hold for 20-30 seconds.
Rest and repeat 2-3 times.
precautions: Ensure desk is stable
Keep breathing -- do not hold your breath
source: Core stability training for office workers

title: Ear-to-Shoulder Neck Stretch
description: A simple lateral neck stretch that targets the scalene muscles and upper trapezius. One of the quickest ways to relieve neck tension during a busy day.
steps: Sit upright with relaxed shoulders.
Slowly tilt your right ear toward your right shoulder.
Keep your left shoulder pressing down -- do not let it rise.
Hold for 15-20 seconds, feeling the stretch on the left side of your neck.
For added stretch, place your right hand gently on the left side of your head.
Return to center. Repeat on the other side.
precautions: Do not pull your head forcefully
Keep the movement gentle

title: Forearm Plank on Desk
description: An elevated plank variation using the desk, making it accessible even for those who cannot do a floor plank. Strengthens the core muscles that support upright posture.
steps: Place both forearms flat on your desk, elbows under shoulders.
Walk your feet back until your body is in a straight diagonal line.
Keep your core tight and back flat.
Look at the desk between your hands.
Hold for 15-30 seconds.
Walk feet forward to stand up. Rest 10 seconds, repeat 3 times.
precautions: Do not hold your breath
Maintain a neutral spine throughout

title: Seated Glute Squeeze
description: An invisible exercise that activates the gluteal muscles while seated. Prolonged sitting causes glute amnesia -- the muscles forget how to fire. This exercise wakes them up.
steps: Sit upright in your chair with feet flat on the floor.
Squeeze your glute muscles (buttocks) as hard as you can.
Hold the squeeze for 5 seconds.
Release for 2 seconds.
Repeat 15-20 times.
Try to initiate the squeeze from deep within the muscle.
precautions: None -- this is one of the safest exercises
source: Muscle activation research for sedentary individuals

title: Scapular Retraction
description: Squeezing the shoulder blades together to activate the rhomboids and middle trapezius. Directly counteracts the protracted (forward-pulled) shoulder position of desk work.
steps: Sit or stand with your arms at your sides.
Pull your shoulder blades together and back, as if trying to hold a pencil between them.
Keep your shoulders down (don't let them shrug up).
Hold the squeeze for 5-8 seconds.
Release slowly.
Repeat 10-12 times.
precautions: Don't arch your lower back while squeezing

title: Wrist Extensor Stretch
description: Targets the extensor muscles on the top of the forearm that are constantly active during mouse use. A critical stretch for anyone who uses a computer mouse extensively.
steps: Extend your right arm in front of you, palm facing down.
Use your left hand to gently bend your right wrist downward, fingers pointing to the floor.
Keep your right arm straight.
Hold for 15-20 seconds.
Switch to the other hand.
Repeat 2-3 times per hand.
benefits: Relieves forearm tension from mouse use
precautions: Stretch gently -- do not force
Stop if you feel sharp pain or tingling

title: Standing Forward Fold
description: A standing forward bend that decompresses the entire spine and stretches the hamstrings. Allows gravity to gently traction the spine after hours of compression from sitting.
steps: Stand with feet hip-width apart.
Slowly roll down vertebra by vertebra, letting your head and arms hang.
Let gravity pull you down -- do not force the stretch.
Bend your knees slightly if your hamstrings are very tight.
Hold for 20-30 seconds, breathing deeply.
Slowly roll back up to standing, stacking each vertebra.
precautions: Bend knees if needed
Rise slowly to avoid dizziness
source: Yoga fundamentals adapted for office workers

title: Physiological Sigh
description: A double-inhale followed by a long exhale, discovered by Stanford neuroscientist Dr. Andrew Huberman to be the fastest known way to reduce stress in real time. Takes just one breath cycle.
steps: Take a sharp inhale through your nose until your lungs are about half full.
Immediately take a second sharp inhale to fill your lungs completely.
Slowly exhale through your mouth for a long, extended breath (as slow as you can).
You should feel an immediate calming effect.
Repeat 1-3 times as needed.
Can be done anywhere without anyone noticing.
precautions: None -- this is a natural physiological mechanism that your body already uses during sleep
source: Dr. Andrew Huberman, Stanford Neuroscience Lab

title: Seated Marching
description: Alternately lifting your knees while seated, as if marching in place. A subtle way to get blood moving without leaving your chair, especially useful on airplane flights or in open offices.
steps: Sit upright with feet flat on the floor.
Lift your right knee toward your chest as high as comfortable.
Lower it back down and immediately lift your left knee.
Continue alternating in a marching rhythm.
Pump your arms gently for added cardio effect.
Continue for 30-60 seconds.
precautions: Maintain upright posture while marching

title: Tricep Desk Dips
description: Using a stable desk or chair to perform tricep dips. Strengthens the back of the arms and engages the core. A quick upper body exercise that pairs well with desk push-ups.
steps: Sit on the edge of a stable desk or sturdy chair.
Place your hands on the edge beside your hips, fingers forward.
Walk your feet forward and lift your hips off the desk.
Lower your body by bending your elbows to about 90 degrees.
Push back up to the starting position.
Perform 8-12 repetitions.
precautions: Ensure desk/chair is stable and won't slide
Avoid if you have shoulder problems

title: Eye Rolling Exercise
description: Systematic eye movements in circular patterns to exercise the extraocular muscles. Helps reduce eye strain from fixed screen staring and improves eye muscle coordination.
steps: Sit comfortably and look straight ahead.
Without moving your head, look up as far as comfortable.
Slowly roll your eyes clockwise in a full circle.
Make the circle as wide as possible without straining.
Complete 5 clockwise circles.
Repeat 5 circles counter-clockwise.
Close your eyes and rest for 10 seconds.
best_for: end of work day eye care
precautions: Move smoothly, not jerkily
Stop if you feel dizzy or nauseous

title: Reverse Prayer Hands
description: Pressing the backs of hands together behind your back to stretch the forearm flexors and open the chest simultaneously. A challenging stretch that addresses multiple desk-work issues at once.
steps: Stand or sit upright.
Bring your hands behind your back.
Press the backs of your hands together with fingers pointing downward.
Try to work your hands up between your shoulder blades.
If full reverse prayer is too difficult, simply interlace fingers behind your back.
Hold for 15-20 seconds.
precautions: Do not force if wrists or shoulders are too tight
Use the easier interlace variation if needed

title: Abdominal Bracing
description: Activating the deep core muscles (transversus abdominis) by bracing as if you were about to be punched in the stomach. An invisible exercise that builds the muscular corset supporting your spine.
steps: Sit upright with feet flat on the floor.
Breathe normally and relax your shoulders.
Tighten your abdominal muscles as if bracing for a punch.
Do not suck in your stomach -- think about widening and stiffening your midsection.
Hold the brace for 10 seconds while continuing to breathe.
Release for 5 seconds. Repeat 8-10 times.
precautions: Keep breathing throughout -- do not hold your breath
source: Spinal stability research by Dr. Stuart McGill

title: Seated Eagle Arms
description: Adapted from yoga's eagle pose (Garudasana), this arm position creates a deep stretch between the shoulder blades and in the upper back. Targets the rhomboids and posterior deltoids.
steps: Sit upright and extend both arms in front of you.
Cross your right arm over your left at the elbows.
Bend your elbows and try to bring your palms together (or as close as possible).
Lift your elbows to shoulder height while keeping shoulders down.
You should feel a deep stretch between your shoulder blades.
Hold for 20-30 seconds. Switch which arm is on top and repeat.
precautions: If palms won't touch, grab opposite shoulders instead
Avoid if you have shoulder injuries

title: Restorative Breath Hold (CO2 Tolerance)
description: Controlled breath holds to improve CO2 tolerance, which reduces the sensitivity to stress and the urge to over-breathe. Used by athletes and high performers to build stress resilience.
steps: Sit quietly and breathe normally for 30 seconds.
Take a normal breath in (not a deep breath).
Exhale normally.
Pinch your nose and hold your breath.
Time how long until you feel the first urge to breathe (this is your BOLT score).
Resume breathing gently through your nose.
Rest 1-2 minutes, then repeat 3-4 times trying to extend the hold slightly.
precautions: Do not push past strong discomfort
Avoid if you have respiratory conditions
Not for during pregnancy
source: Buteyko breathing method and oxygen advantage training

title: Nerve Gliding for Carpal Tunnel
description: Specific movements that glide the median nerve through the carpal tunnel, preventing adhesions and reducing numbness/tingling. A targeted exercise for developers and heavy computer users.
steps: Start with a fist, wrist neutral.
Extend your fingers straight, keeping wrist neutral.
Extend your wrist back (fingers pointing up), fingers straight.
With wrist extended, extend your thumb out to the side.
With everything extended, use your other hand to gently pull your thumb further back.
Hold each position for 5 seconds. Repeat the full sequence 5 times per hand.
precautions: Movements should be gentle and pain-free
See a doctor if symptoms persist
source: Nerve mobilization protocols from hand surgery rehabilitation

title: Seated Toe Touches
description: A simple forward fold while seated that stretches the entire posterior chain -- from the lower back through the hamstrings. Provides a moment of relaxation and decompression.
steps: Push your chair back from the desk slightly.
Sit with feet flat on the floor, wider than hip-width.
Exhale and slowly fold forward between your knees.
Let your head, neck, and arms hang heavy.
Reach your hands toward the floor or grasp your ankles.
Hold for 15-20 seconds, breathing deeply. Slowly roll back up.
precautions: Rise slowly to avoid dizziness
Skip if you have low blood pressure issues

title: Resistance Band Rows (with Band)
description: Using a resistance band looped around a desk leg or door handle to perform rowing motions. Directly strengthens the muscles that pull your shoulders back into proper posture.
steps: Attach a resistance band to a desk leg or door handle at elbow height.
Hold both ends of the band, arms extended in front of you.
Sit or stand with good posture.
Pull the band toward your body, squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Slowly return to the starting position with control.
Perform 12-15 repetitions for 2-3 sets.
best_for: those ready for progression beyond bodyweight
precautions: Ensure band is securely anchored
Start with light resistance

title: Micro-Walk (2-Minute Walk Break)
description: Simply walking for 2 minutes every 30-60 minutes of sitting. Research shows that even brief walking breaks significantly reduce cardiovascular risk and improve cognitive function compared to uninterrupted sitting.
steps: Set a timer for every 30-60 minutes.
When it goes off, stand up from your chair.
Walk at a comfortable pace -- to the kitchen, bathroom, around the office floor.
Take at least 100 steps (about 2 minutes).
Walk briskly if possible to increase the benefit.
Return to your desk refreshed.
precautions: None -- walking is the most fundamental human movement
source: Journal of the American Medical Association, 2023 study on sitting breaks

title: 20-20-20 Eye Rule Exercise
description: Every 20 minutes, look at something 20 feet away for 20 seconds. The simplest and most widely recommended eye exercise for preventing digital eye strain.
steps: Set a reminder for every 20 minutes of screen time.
When the reminder goes off, pause your work.
Look at an object at least 20 feet (6 meters) away -- a window view is ideal.
Focus on the distant object for a full 20 seconds.
Blink several times slowly and fully.
Return to your screen.
precautions: See an optometrist if eye strain persists despite this habit

title: Full Body Desk Stretch Sequence
description: A comprehensive 5-minute sequence combining the most effective desk stretches into one flowing routine. Perfect for a mid-morning and mid-afternoon break.
steps: Neck rolls: 5 slow circles each direction (30 sec).
Shoulder shrugs: 8 reps with 3-second holds (30 sec).
Chest opener: clasp hands behind back, lift and squeeze (30 sec).
Seated spinal twist: 20 seconds each side (45 sec).
Wrist circles and prayer stretch (30 sec).
Seated cat-cow: 8 repetitions with breath (45 sec).
Figure-4 hip stretch: 20 seconds each side (45 sec).
Standing forward fold with bent knees (30 sec).
3 deep diaphragmatic breaths to finish (15 sec).
precautions: Skip any movement that causes pain
Modify depth based on your flexibility
source: Compiled from physical therapy and ergonomic best practices
